warnings.advisory: Skill called MCP tools but the rubric has no dimension name suggesting tool-usage coverage (matched against keywords: ['tool usage', 'tool use', 'tool work', 'tool call', 'argument quality', 'argument-quality', 'response interpretation', 'tool selection', 'mcp tool', 'fixture']). The judge will grade other dimensions but won't score tool work explicitly. Consider adding a tool-usage dimension or renaming an existing one.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: Assertions are factually accurate. The skill correctly extracted all stated facts: name, age, birthplace, occupation for Thomas; name, age, birthplace for Mary; name, age, birthplace for Patrick. Birth years were correctly computed as indirect evidence from stated ages. The skill properly flagged the birthplace conflict (Ireland vs. Pennsylvania objective) rather than fabricating resolution. No fabricated material or unsupported claims.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted all required information from the 1850 census record. For each of the three household members (Thomas, Mary, Patrick), it created separate atomic assertions covering name, age, birth year, birthplace, sex, and residence. Thomas's occupation was extracted. Relationship assertions for Mary (as wife) and Patrick (as child) were correctly created as indirect evidence. No omissions of data present in the source.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: All three MCP tool calls used correct arguments. Log append (mcp__genealogy__research_log_append) was called with appropriate description and outcome. Tree edit (mcp__genealogy__tree_edit) correctly added source with valid title. Research append (mcp__genealogy__research_append) batched all 22 operations (1 source + 21 assertions) with correct structure, section names, and entry formats. No fixture_not_found errors; all calls matched live and validated successfully.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Every assertion represents a single extractable fact. Name, age, birth (computed from age), birthplace, occupation, residence, sex, and relationship are each in separate a_ entries with their own fact_type. Compound facts like 'Thomas Flynn, age 32, miner' are decomposed across a_001 (name), a_002 (age), a_006 (occupation). Downstream skills can query individual facts without parsing compound values.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly distinguishes informant from recorder. For name/age/birthplace facts on adult household members (Thomas, Mary) and child Patrick, informant is identified as 'unknown household member, likely Thomas/Mary/parent' with informant_proximity as 'household_member'—someone in the household had to answer. For residence and sex (observed), informant is 'census enumerator' with informant_proximity 'witness', properly separating the recorder (enumerator) from the information provider (household member for reported facts).

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Evidence types are correctly assigned per 1850 census standards. Direct evidence: stated facts (name, age, birthplace, occupation, sex) reported to the enumerator. Indirect evidence: birth year (computed from age via arithmetic, requiring calculation); relationship facts for Mary and Patrick (inferred from household position, not explicitly stated—the 1850 census has no relationship column). The skill accurately notes this distinction and classifies relationship as indirect per the pre-1880 census standard.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All substantive claims are accurate and supported. The skill correctly identified that Bridget and John needed stubs (Mary and James already exist in tree), created two new persons (I5, I6) with correct gender/names, extracted 17 assertions matching the source data, and added two ParentChild relationships (R4, R5) to Thomas. No fabrications; all facts from the 1850 census are correctly transcribed. Tree state matches expected: I1 Patrick, I2 Thomas, I3 Mary, I4 James exist; I5 Bridget and I6 John were added with proper relationships.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Skill addressed all required work: (1) extracted assertions for all four household members (Thomas—5 assertions on age/birth/birthplace/occupation/residence; Bridget—6 on name/age/birth/birthplace/relationship/residence; Patrick—already covered; John—6 on name/age/birth/birthplace/relationship/residence); (2) detected Patrick in child_2 role with Thomas (I2) as parent, triggering sibling stub logic; (3) correctly identified Bridget and John as new siblings not in tree; (4) skipped Mary (I3) and James (I4) already present; (5) created person stubs with tree_edit for I5, I6; (6) added ParentChild relationships R4, R5; (7) did not duplicate Patrick or Thomas. All required work completed.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All three tool calls passed correct arguments. First tree_edit: added two persons with gender (Female/Male), names with given/surname/type/preferred—matches expected person stub schema. Second research_append: 18 nested ops (1 source update + 17 assertions) with all required fields (source_id, record_role, fact_type, value, informant, evidence_type, etc.)—schema valid per response validation. Third tree_edit: added two ParentChild relationships with type, parent (I2), child (I5, I6), sources—correct structure. No fixture_not_found errors; matched.kind = 'live' on all calls.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Each assertion extracts a single, decomposable fact. Thomas age is separate from birth year (calculated), which is separate from birthplace—not bundled. Bridget's name (given + surname structured separately) is distinct from her age, which is distinct from birthplace. Relationship and residence are each standalone assertions. Compound data (e.g., 'age 7') is stored in value but structured_value isolates the number; calculated birth years derive atomically from age. No assertions crammed with multiple fact_types; downstream skills can query individual facts.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Informant identification
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: For Thomas's facts (head of household): informant identified as 'Unknown household member (likely Thomas Flynn himself)' with informant_proximity 'household_member' for age/birthplace/occupation, and 'witness' for residence (enumerator). For Bridget and John (children): informant 'Unknown household member (likely a parent)' with proximity 'household_member' for name/age/birth/birthplace/relationship, and 'witness' for residence. Critically, the skill distinguished the actual reporter (household member/parent) from the recorder (enumerator), as required by the rubric.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Evidence types correctly assigned per 1850 census source constraints. Direct evidence: name, age (as stated), birthplace, occupation, residence—all stated explicitly in the record. Indirect evidence: birth year calculated from age (not directly stated in record). Relationship marked indirect because 1850 census has no relationship column (introduced 1880)—relationship inferred from household position and shared surname, not explicitly stated. All classifications match the rubric's requirement that 1850 co-residence without explicit relationship column yields indirect evidence for kinship.

output.text_response:
**Reading both files — done.** Here's the state snapshot before writing anything:

**Existing sources:** src_001–src_009 → next source is **src_010** (but src_001 covers this 1850 FamilySearch record; I'll reuse and update it)
**Existing assertions:** a_001–a_013 → next is **a_014**
**Tree persons:** I1 Patrick, I2 Thomas, I3 Mary (F), I4 James (M)
**Tree relationships:** R1 I2→I1, R2 I2→I3, R3 I2→I4

**Sibling enumeration checklist (required before writing stubs):**
- Parents in tree: Thomas Flynn = **I2** ✓ → trigger fires
- Bridget Flynn (F): tree has I3 Mary Flynn (F) — name mismatch → **create stub**
- John Flynn (M): tree has I4 James Flynn (M) — name mismatch → **create stub**

**Roles to extract fully:**
| Role | Person | Already extracted |
|------|--------|-------------------|
| head_of_household | Thomas (32, M, Laborer, Ireland) | name only (a_005) — need age, birthplace, occupation, residence |
| child_1 | Bridget (7, F, Ireland) | nothing — full extraction needed |
| child_2 | Patrick (5, M, Ireland) | a_001–a_004 cover this ✓ |
| child_3 | John (2, M, Pennsylvania) | nothing — full extraction needed |

Now making the first tree_edit (person stubs) and research_append (assertions) in parallel, since S1 already exists and research_append has no dependency on new person IDs:Stubs assigned: **Bridget Flynn = I5**, **John Flynn = I6**. Now adding ParentChild edges to Thomas (I2):All writes confirmed. Here's the terse summary:

---

**Source:** src_001 updated (corrected ARK to `MXYZ-TP4`); GedcomX S1 reused.

**Assertions added — 17 new (a_014–a_030):**
- `head_of_household` (Thomas): age, birth year ~1818, birthplace Ireland, occupation Laborer, residence — **5 assertions**
- `child_1` (Bridget): name, age, birth year ~1843, birthplace Ireland, relationship, residence — **6 assertions**
- `child_3` (John): name, age, birth year ~1848, birthplace Pennsylvania, relationship, residence — **6 assertions**
- *(Patrick child_2 already covered by a_001–a_004; Thomas name by a_005)*

**Tree changes:**
- I5 Bridget Flynn (F) created → R4 I2→I5 ParentChild added
- I6 John Flynn (M) created → R5 I2→I6 ParentChild added
- Thomas (I2) now has 4 children in tree: Patrick (I1), Mary (I3), James (I4), Bridget (I5), John (I6)

**Key finding:** John's Pennsylvania birthplace (vs. Ireland for Thomas, Bridget, and Patrick) places the family's emigration between ~1843 and ~1848 — useful context for q_001. No mother/wife listed in this household; her absence is notable and may warrant a negative assertion if expected.

**Next:** Run `check-warnings` on I5 and I6, then `person-evidence` to link Bridget and John's assertions to their new person IDs.

warnings.advisory: Skill called MCP tools but the rubric has no dimension name suggesting tool-usage coverage (matched against keywords: ['tool usage', 'tool use', 'tool work', 'tool call', 'argument quality', 'argument-quality', 'response interpretation', 'tool selection', 'mcp tool', 'fixture']). The judge will grade other dimensions but won't score tool work explicitly. Consider adding a tool-usage dimension or renaming an existing one.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: All substantive claims are accurate and supported. The skill correctly identified that Bridget and John needed stubs (Mary and James already exist in tree), created two new persons (I5, I6) with correct gender/names, extracted 17 assertions matching the source data, and added two ParentChild relationships (R4, R5) to Thomas. No fabrications; all facts from the 1850 census are correctly transcribed. Tree state matches expected: I1 Patrick, I2 Thomas, I3 Mary, I4 James exist; I5 Bridget and I6 John were added with proper relationships.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: Skill addressed all required work: (1) extracted assertions for all four household members (Thomas—5 assertions on age/birth/birthplace/occupation/residence; Bridget—6 on name/age/birth/birthplace/relationship/residence; Patrick—already covered; John—6 on name/age/birth/birthplace/relationship/residence); (2) detected Patrick in child_2 role with Thomas (I2) as parent, triggering sibling stub logic; (3) correctly identified Bridget and John as new siblings not in tree; (4) skipped Mary (I3) and James (I4) already present; (5) created person stubs with tree_edit for I5, I6; (6) added ParentChild relationships R4, R5; (7) did not duplicate Patrick or Thomas. All required work completed.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: All three tool calls passed correct arguments. First tree_edit: added two persons with gender (Female/Male), names with given/surname/type/preferred—matches expected person stub schema. Second research_append: 18 nested ops (1 source update + 17 assertions) with all required fields (source_id, record_role, fact_type, value, informant, evidence_type, etc.)—schema valid per response validation. Third tree_edit: added two ParentChild relationships with type, parent (I2), child (I5, I6), sources—correct structure. No fixture_not_found errors; matched.kind = 'live' on all calls.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Each assertion extracts a single, decomposable fact. Thomas age is separate from birth year (calculated), which is separate from birthplace—not bundled. Bridget's name (given + surname structured separately) is distinct from her age, which is distinct from birthplace. Relationship and residence are each standalone assertions. Compound data (e.g., 'age 7') is stored in value but structured_value isolates the number; calculated birth years derive atomically from age. No assertions crammed with multiple fact_types; downstream skills can query individual facts.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: For Thomas's facts (head of household): informant identified as 'Unknown household member (likely Thomas Flynn himself)' with informant_proximity 'household_member' for age/birthplace/occupation, and 'witness' for residence (enumerator). For Bridget and John (children): informant 'Unknown household member (likely a parent)' with proximity 'household_member' for name/age/birth/birthplace/relationship, and 'witness' for residence. Critically, the skill distinguished the actual reporter (household member/parent) from the recorder (enumerator), as required by the rubric.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Evidence types correctly assigned per 1850 census source constraints. Direct evidence: name, age (as stated), birthplace, occupation, residence—all stated explicitly in the record. Indirect evidence: birth year calculated from age (not directly stated in record). Relationship marked indirect because 1850 census has no relationship column (introduced 1880)—relationship inferred from household position and shared surname, not explicitly stated. All classifications match the rubric's requirement that 1850 co-residence without explicit relationship column yields indirect evidence for kinship.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Mostly factually correct extraction with proper citations and informant identification. However, the death date (a_005) and death place (a_006) are attributed to Dr. William H. Stein with informant_proximity 'official_duty', which is partially incorrect. While the physician certified the cause of death, the informant Mary Flynn reported the death date and place; the physician's role was medical certification, not reporting these basic facts. Additionally, the burial date (a_019) attribution to J.J. Franey as undertaker may be inferential rather than directly attested in the certificate text provided. The computed birth year (a_007) with value '~1845' violates the instruction to avoid computing exact dates from age; the note acknowledges '~January 2, 1845' which is an exact date inference that should not appear. These are notable but not systematic misattributions.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Skill extracted all substantive facts from the certificate: name, sex, race, death date/place, age, birthplace, occupation, parents' names and birthplaces, cause of death, illness duration, burial date/place, and informant details. No significant facts were omitted from the source document provided.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All three MCP tool calls passed valid arguments matching expected behavior. research_log_append received appropriate query/outcome fields; tree_edit add_source operation was correct; research_append correctly batched source and 20 assertions with proper section/op structure. No fixture_not_found errors. Arguments align with tool requirements.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Each assertion represents a single extractable fact. Death date (a_005) and death place (a_006) are properly separated; age (a_007) and birth year (a_008) are separate; parents' names, birthplaces are atomized into individual assertions (a_011–a_015); burial date (a_019) and place (a_020) are distinct. No compound facts crammed into single assertions.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Informant identification
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Informant identification is mostly correct and well-reasoned. Mary Flynn is correctly identified as the named informant for personal/family facts with proper proximity distinctions (witness vs. family_not_present). Dr. Stein is identified for medical facts. However, the death date and place (a_005–a_006) are misattributed to the physician with 'official_duty' proximity when Mary Flynn (as informant) is the source of these facts; the physician's role was certification, not reporting them. The per-test context states 'Mary Flynn may have reported the death date/place but the physician certified the medical cause.' This represents a meaningful mischaracterization of informant roles for two key death facts.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Most evidence types are correctly assigned. Birth facts (parents' names/places, birthplace) correctly marked indirect per BCG standards. However, age (a_007) is marked 'direct' when it should be 'indirect'—the certificate states age, not a recorded birth date; age is the informant's claim, not direct documentation. The birth year (a_008) is correctly marked indirect. Birthplace (a_009) is marked 'direct' but should arguably be 'indirect' given it is reported secondhand by the wife, not directly witnessed. The skill's rationale acknowledges secondhand reporting in informant_bias_notes but contradicts this by assigning 'direct' evidence_type.

output.text_response:
Reading both project files up front, then proceeding.Got `log_001` and `S1`. No sibling stubs needed (Patrick is `deceased`, not `child_N`). Now batching source + 20 assertions in one call.**Extraction complete — src_001 / S1 · a_001–a_020 · log_001**

| Role | Assertions | IDs |
|---|---|---|
| `deceased` (Patrick Flynn) | 17 | a_001–a_017 |
| `informant` (Mary Flynn, wife) | 3 | a_018–a_020 |

**Key findings:**
- **Birthplace conflict:** Certificate states Ireland; project objective assumed Pennsylvania — warrants investigation.
- **Parents named:** Father Thomas Flynn (b. Ireland), Mother Mary Brennan (b. Ireland) — both `indirect` evidence per BCG standards (wife reported secondhand).
- **Three informants classified:** Dr. William H. Stein (`official_duty`) for death/medical facts; Mary Flynn (`witness`/`family_not_present`) for personal/family facts; J.J. Franey (`official_duty`) for burial facts.
- **Birth year a_007:** Calculated ~1845 from stated age 63y 2m 10d — `indirect`, `calculated`.

**Next steps:** Run `check-warnings` for plausibility checks on I1, then `person-evidence` to link a_001–a_017 to I1 and create a stub for wife Mary Flynn.

added.informant_bias_notes: Precise age (63 years, 2 months, 10 days) implies knowledge of birth date; wife did not witness the decedent's birth and is reporting secondhand knowledge of his birthdate.

Birth year calculated from stated age (63 years, 2 months, 10 days) at death on March 12, 1908; computed approximate birth ca. January 2, 1845. Classified indirect: arithmetic inference from stated age, not a directly recorded birth fact.

Wife did not witness decedent's birth in Ireland; reporting secondhand knowledge. Note: project objective states Patrick Flynn 'born ca. 1845 in Pennsylvania'; this record states Ireland — potential conflict requiring further investigation.

Death certificate parents' names are indirect evidence (BCG standard): the personal informant reports what she was told, not events she witnessed. Wife likely learned the father's name from the decedent himself.
Father's birthplace is indirect evidence on a death certificate; wife reports secondhand knowledge of a place and time she did not witness.
Mother's maiden name is indirect evidence on a death certificate. Record specifies maiden name 'Brennan'. Wife reports secondhand knowledge of the decedent's mother.
Mother's birthplace is indirect evidence on a death certificate; wife reports secondhand knowledge.

warnings.advisory: Skill called MCP tools but the rubric has no dimension name suggesting tool-usage coverage (matched against keywords: ['tool usage', 'tool use', 'tool work', 'tool call', 'argument quality', 'argument-quality', 'response interpretation', 'tool selection', 'mcp tool', 'fixture']). The judge will grade other dimensions but won't score tool work explicitly. Consider adding a tool-usage dimension or renaming an existing one.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: Mostly factually correct extraction with proper citations and informant identification. However, the death date (a_005) and death place (a_006) are attributed to Dr. William H. Stein with informant_proximity 'official_duty', which is partially incorrect. While the physician certified the cause of death, the informant Mary Flynn reported the death date and place; the physician's role was medical certification, not reporting these basic facts. Additionally, the burial date (a_019) attribution to J.J. Franey as undertaker may be inferential rather than directly attested in the certificate text provided. The computed birth year (a_007) with value '~1845' violates the instruction to avoid computing exact dates from age; the note acknowledges '~January 2, 1845' which is an exact date inference that should not appear. These are notable but not systematic misattributions.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: Skill extracted all substantive facts from the certificate: name, sex, race, death date/place, age, birthplace, occupation, parents' names and birthplaces, cause of death, illness duration, burial date/place, and informant details. No significant facts were omitted from the source document provided.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: All three MCP tool calls passed valid arguments matching expected behavior. research_log_append received appropriate query/outcome fields; tree_edit add_source operation was correct; research_append correctly batched source and 20 assertions with proper section/op structure. No fixture_not_found errors. Arguments align with tool requirements.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Each assertion represents a single extractable fact. Death date (a_005) and death place (a_006) are properly separated; age (a_007) and birth year (a_008) are separate; parents' names, birthplaces are atomized into individual assertions (a_011–a_015); burial date (a_019) and place (a_020) are distinct. No compound facts crammed into single assertions.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: Informant identification is mostly correct and well-reasoned. Mary Flynn is correctly identified as the named informant for personal/family facts with proper proximity distinctions (witness vs. family_not_present). Dr. Stein is identified for medical facts. However, the death date and place (a_005–a_006) are misattributed to the physician with 'official_duty' proximity when Mary Flynn (as informant) is the source of these facts; the physician's role was certification, not reporting them. The per-test context states 'Mary Flynn may have reported the death date/place but the physician certified the medical cause.' This represents a meaningful mischaracterization of informant roles for two key death facts.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Most evidence types are correctly assigned. Birth facts (parents' names/places, birthplace) correctly marked indirect per BCG standards. However, age (a_007) is marked 'direct' when it should be 'indirect'—the certificate states age, not a recorded birth date; age is the informant's claim, not direct documentation. The birth year (a_008) is correctly marked indirect. Birthplace (a_009) is marked 'direct' but should arguably be 'indirect' given it is reported secondhand by the wife, not directly witnessed. The skill's rationale acknowledges secondhand reporting in informant_bias_notes but contradicts this by assigning 'direct' evidence_type.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All extracted assertions accurately reflect the marriage record content. Facts are properly sourced and supported: groom/bride names, ages, birthplaces, occupations, parent names, marriage date/place, and officiant information all match the provided record. The skill correctly identified Patrick's birthplace as Ireland and flagged it as contradicting the project objective (Pennsylvania), which is appropriate conflict identification. The birth year calculations (1845, 1848) are correctly marked as indirect evidence with explanations. All 22 assertions are factually accurate to the source.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted all required information from the marriage record. Both groom (Patrick Flynn) and bride (Catherine Gallagher) are fully documented with name, age, birthplace, residence, and parents. The marriage event itself (date, place, officiant) is captured. Witnesses (Thomas Flynn, James Gallagher) are noted as FAN associates with identifying facts. Parent names for both parties are extracted. The source is properly cited and logged. No substantive information from the record was omitted.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All three MCP tool calls passed with correct arguments and matched 'live' status. The research_log_append call properly captured record_type ('marriage_license_certificate'), license_no ('4872'), location, names, date, and outcome. The tree_edit call correctly used 'add_source' operation with appropriate title. The research_append call correctly structured 23 operation entries (1 source + 22 assertions) with all required fields (section, op, entry) and valid JSON. No fixture_not_found errors occurred.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Each assertion is a single, extractable fact. For example: name, age, birth (year), birth (place), residence, and occupation are separate assertions rather than combined. Relationship assertions for parents list only the relationship type and role, not compound facts. The skill decomposed facts appropriately—marriage date and place are in a single marriage assertion (correct, as they are part of the same event) but fact types like age, residence, and occupation are atomized. This structure enables downstream skills to query individual facts effectively.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Informant identification
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Informants are specifically identified with proper proximity assessment. For Patrick's and Catherine's personal facts (name, age, birthplace, residence, occupation, parents), the informant is correctly identified as the respective party (groom/bride) with proximity 'self'. For the marriage event (date, place), the informant is Rev. Daniel O'Connor (officiant) with proximity 'official_duty'. The skill distinguishes the actual informant (who provided the information) from the recorder (implied clerk), meeting the standard that household members reported their own facts on the license.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Evidence types are correctly assigned throughout. Direct evidence is used for: name, age, birthplace, residence, occupation, parent names (all explicitly stated on the record), marriage date/place, and officiant name. Indirect evidence is used for birth year (~1845, ~1848), which are correctly noted as computed from stated age minus marriage year—a valid inference requiring explanation. The skill provided clear rationale for why birth years are indirect ('the year is computed, not stated'). All classifications align with whether facts are explicitly stated versus inferred from arithmetic.

output.text_response:
Reading project state first, then I'll fire the log entry and source description simultaneously.Got `log_001` and `S1`. Now batching all 23 ops — source + 22 assertions — in one `research_append` call.All 23 ops written successfully. Here's the terse summary:

---

**Source:** `src_001` · `S1` — *Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania Marriage License and Certificate, 1870* (original)

**Assertions by role** (a_001–a_022, 22 total):
| Role | Count | Key facts |
|---|---|---|
| `groom` | 9 | name, age, birth yr ~1845 (indirect), birthplace, residence, occupation, father, mother, marriage |
| `bride` | 8 | name, age, birth yr ~1848 (indirect), birthplace, residence, father, mother, marriage |
| `father_of_groom` | 1 | Thomas Flynn |
| `mother_of_groom` | 1 | Mary Brennan |
| `father_of_bride` | 1 | James Gallagher |
| `mother_of_bride` | 1 | Bridget Dolan |
| `officiant` | 1 | Rev. Daniel O'Connor |

**Tree changes:** `S1` added; no new persons (sibling-stub trigger does not apply — Patrick is `groom`, not `child_N`).

**⚠ Conflict to flag:** The project objective describes Patrick as "born ca. 1845 in **Pennsylvania**," but this marriage license — reported by Patrick himself (`self`, `primary`) — states his birthplace as **Ireland**. This direct contradiction should be routed to `conflict-resolution` before closing the parents question.

**Key finding:** Thomas Flynn named as Patrick's father on the license **and** appears as `witness_1` in person — dual corroboration strengthens the parent identification.

**Next steps:** Run `person-evidence` to link `groom` assertions to `I1`, then `conflict-resolution` on the Pennsylvania vs. Ireland birthplace discrepancy.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: All extracted assertions accurately reflect the marriage record content. Facts are properly sourced and supported: groom/bride names, ages, birthplaces, occupations, parent names, marriage date/place, and officiant information all match the provided record. The skill correctly identified Patrick's birthplace as Ireland and flagged it as contradicting the project objective (Pennsylvania), which is appropriate conflict identification. The birth year calculations (1845, 1848) are correctly marked as indirect evidence with explanations. All 22 assertions are factually accurate to the source.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted all required information from the marriage record. Both groom (Patrick Flynn) and bride (Catherine Gallagher) are fully documented with name, age, birthplace, residence, and parents. The marriage event itself (date, place, officiant) is captured. Witnesses (Thomas Flynn, James Gallagher) are noted as FAN associates with identifying facts. Parent names for both parties are extracted. The source is properly cited and logged. No substantive information from the record was omitted.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: All three MCP tool calls passed with correct arguments and matched 'live' status. The research_log_append call properly captured record_type ('marriage_license_certificate'), license_no ('4872'), location, names, date, and outcome. The tree_edit call correctly used 'add_source' operation with appropriate title. dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Each assertion is a single, extractable fact. For example: name, age, birth (year), birth (place), residence, and occupation are separate assertions rather than combined. Relationship assertions for parents list only the relationship type and role, not compound facts. The skill decomposed facts appropriately—marriage date and place are in a single marriage assertion (correct, as they are part of the same event) but fact types like age, residence, and occupation are atomized. This structure enables downstream skills to query individual facts effectively.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: Informants are specifically identified with proper proximity assessment. For Patrick's and Catherine's personal facts (name, age, birthplace, residence, occupation, parents), the informant is correctly identified as the respective party (groom/bride) with proximity 'self'. For the marriage event (date, place), the informant is Rev. Daniel O'Connor (officiant) with proximity 'official_duty'. The skill distinguishes the actual informant (who provided the information) from the recorder (implied clerk), meeting the standard that household members reported their own facts on the license.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Evidence types are correctly assigned throughout. Direct evidence is used for: name, age, birthplace, residence, occupation, parent names (all explicitly stated on the record), marriage date/place, and officiant name. Indirect evidence is used for birth year (~1845, ~1848), which are correctly noted as computed from stated age minus marriage year—a valid inference requiring explanation. The skill provided clear rationale for why birth years are indirect ('the year is computed, not stated'). All classifications align with whether facts are explicitly stated versus inferred from arithmetic.

| | |
|---|---|

**Key finding:** Patrick Flynn not located in 1870 Schuylkill County census (expected ~age 25). Analytically significant: he was enumerated there in 1850 and 1860. Bias notes document four alternative explanations (relocation, enumerator miss, indexing gap, damaged pages).

- Continue the 1880 and 1900 census searches to close the `t_001` timeline gap

dimensions.rationale: The single assertion (a_014) is atomic: it represents one extractable fact — Patrick Flynn's absence from the 1870 census — with a single fact_type (residence) and record_role (absent). dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly identified the informant as 'Researcher' rather than a household member or census enumerator, which is the correct approach for negative evidence. The informant_proximity field is set to 'unknown' with explicit reasoning: the analyst (researcher) concluded absence after exhaustive search, not a household member providing positive testimony. The bias_notes clearly distinguish the analyst's role from what would apply to positive census assertions, meeting the requirement to assess proximity to the event in the context of negative evidence.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: The evidence_type 'negative' is correctly assigned. The assertion documents the absence of Patrick Flynn from the 1870 census population schedule despite an exhaustive search — this is the textbook definition of negative evidence. The record_role 'absent' reinforces that the finding is absence, not presence with inferred relationships. The per-test context confirms this is the correct classification for a nil search result.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy

output.text_response:
Reading both project files up front before any other step.Loading the MCP tools needed for persistence.**State:** `research.json` — no sources, no assertions, no log; next source = `src_001`. `tree.gedcomx.json` — only I1 (Patrick Flynn). No other children in the record; Thomas Flynn (P2/parent) not in tree → 5d sibling stubs skipped.

Step 1 (log entry) and Step 5c (tree source description) — firing in parallel.Log: `log_001`. Tree source: `S1`. Now batching `src_001` + 7 assertions, and checking record matches — parallel.The batch call needs top-level `section`/`op` alongside `ops`. Retrying with them present.The validator requires a sidecar when `record_persona_id` is set, but `log_001` was created without a staged ref. I need to run `record_search` fresh with `projectPath` to get a staged handle. Loading that tool first.`record_search` isn't available as a standalone tool here, so I can't produce a fresh staged ref. The clean fix: set `record_persona_id: null` on all assertions (same as a `record_read` path) — no sidecar required, data fully valid. Resubmitting the full batch.All writes succeeded. Here's the summary:

---

**Source:** `src_001` / `S1` — 1850 U.S. Federal Census, original, FamilySearch

**Assertions — 7 total (a_001–a_007)**
- `child_1` (Patrick Flynn, P1) — 5 assertions: name (direct), birth year ~1845 (indirect — age-derived), birthplace Ireland (direct), residence Branch Township Schuylkill PA 1850 (direct), relationship child_inferred of Thomas Flynn (indirect — no 1850 relationship column)
- `head_of_household` (Thomas Flynn, P2) — 2 assertions: name (direct), residence Branch Township Schuylkill PA 1850 (direct)

**Tree changes:** `S1` added; no sibling stubs (no other children in record; Thomas Flynn not yet in tree)

**Record match check (informational):** MXHY-TP4 has an **accepted** match to FamilySearch tree person `LZNY-BRF` "Patrick Flynn" — confidence 5, score 0.97. This record is already attached to someone in the FS tree.

**Note:** `record_persona_id` set to `null` on all assertions — no staged sidecar was available since search-records passed the result in-context without a `projectPath` staged ref. Downstream person-evidence can still link via name/date/place correlation.

**Key finding:** Thomas Flynn (P2, likely father) is not yet in `tree.gedcomx.json` — the ParentChild relationship is inferred but unresolved. This is the primary lead for the research objective.

**Next step:** Run **person-evidence** to link `child_1` assertions → I1, then **check-warnings** for any data inconsistencies.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted 7 assertions, correctly identified the record match (MXHY-TP4 to tree person LZNY-BRF with status 'accepted'), and wrote assertions with the arkUrl as record_id. However, record_persona_id was set to null across all assertions to work around a sidecar validation error, rather than being set to 'P1' as the test expected. The test instructions specified 'record_persona_id set to P1 for the focus person,' but the skill resolved the validation error by nullifying the field instead. This diverges from the expected behavior even though the workaround is defensible given the tool constraints encountered.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill completed all required work: extracted all 7 assertions (name, birth year, birthplace, residence, relationship for Patrick; name and residence for Thomas); checked the record match via record_person_matches and reported the accepted match to LZNY-BRF; appended the source to tree.gedcomx.json; and logged the search. No required work was omitted, despite the technical hurdle with the sidecar requirement.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: The skill made 5 MCP tool calls: (1) research_log_append with correct search query details; (2) tree_edit add_source with title and URL; (3) record_person_matches with id 'MXHY-TP4' matching expected_args exactly; (4) research_append that failed due to sidecar requirement; (5) research_append retry with record_persona_id set to null, which succeeded. All tool calls that matched the fixtures used the correct arguments. The fixture for record_person_matches was explicitly matched, and all other calls proceeded without fixture_not_found errors.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Each assertion is a single extractable fact. Name, birth year, birthplace, and residence are separate assertions with distinct fact_types. The relationship assertion ('child_inferred' of Thomas Flynn) is atomic—one relationship per assertion, not compounded with inference method. The structured_value field in the relationship assertion cleanly separates relationship_type from related_person_role, enabling downstream queries of individual facts.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly identified informants and assessed proximity. For Patrick's name, birth, and birthplace, informant is 'unknown household member (likely a parent)' with proximity 'household_member' and detailed bias notes explaining why a parent would report these facts. For residence, informant is 'census enumerator' with proximity 'witness,' correctly distinguishing the recorder from the actual informant. Thomas Flynn's name similarly uses 'unknown household member (likely self or spouse)' with household_member proximity and reasoning.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Evidence types are correctly assigned. Patrick's name and birthplace are 'direct'—explicitly stated in the record. Birth year (~1845) is 'indirect'—derived by subtracting stated age from census year. The ParentChild relationship is 'indirect' because the 1850 census had no relationship column (as noted in bias_notes); it was inferred by household position. Residence is 'direct' for both Patrick and Thomas. All direct/indirect classifications align with the source's actual content structure and the historical absence of relationship columns in 1850 census records.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All assertions extracted from the record_read response are factually correct and directly supported by the response data. Patrick Flynn's name, birthplace (Ireland), residence (Branch Township, Schuylkill, PA, 1850), and the inferred ParentChild relationship with Thomas Flynn are all accurately captured. The skill correctly identifies the source as the 1850 U.S. Census and properly classifies it as 'original.' No fabricated material; all claims are grounded in the tool response.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted all required assertions: Patrick Flynn's name, birth year (~1845), birthplace (Ireland), residence (1850), and the ParentChild relationship to Thomas Flynn. Also extracted Thomas Flynn's name and residence. A source entry was created. The skill addressed everything the user message and test context required — complete coverage of both the primary person and the household head, plus relationship inference.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The record_read call passed 'ark:/61903/1:1:68Q9-K34P', matching the expected_args pattern '~68Q9-K34P' exactly. The call structure is correct and the critical recordId argument matches the test's expectation. Log append, tree edit, and research append calls used correct tool invocations with valid project paths and operations. No fixture_not_found errors; all calls succeeded with 'kind: predicate' or 'kind: live'.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity

output.text_response:
Good. Now let me read the project files and fetch the record simultaneously.The record has three persons: child (Christian Frederich), father (Tollev Nadnesen), and mother (Inger Larsdatter). Critically, the source note embedded in the record flags "Nadnesen" as uncertain — image tools aren't available here, so the surname will be recorded tentative. Let me log the fetch and write the tree source simultaneously, then batch all assertions.Log → `log_001`, source → `S1`. Now batch everything — source entry + 10 assertions — in one `research_append` call.All 11 ops persisted cleanly. Here's the summary:

---

**src_001 / S1** — "Norway, Church Books, 1797-1958," FamilySearch; original church book; `log_001`

| Role | Assertions | IDs |
|---|---|---|
| child | name, christening, birth date, birthplace | a_001–a_004 |
| father | name ⚠️ tentative, residence, relationship | a_005–a_007 |
| mother | name, residence, relationship | a_008–a_010 |

**Key finding on the father:**
- **Given name: Tollev** — well supported; internally consistent with the subject's own patronymic *Tollefsen* (son of Tollef/Tollev).
- **Surname: `[Nadnesen?]` — recorded TENTATIVE.** The index reads *Nadnesen*, which is not a recognizable Norwegian patronymic. The near-certain explanation is a Kurrent-script misread of *Aadnesen* (the leading *Aa* read as *N*), from *Ådne/Aadne*, a given name common in Rogaland. The original register image must be consulted before this surname can be treated as established.
- **Mother: Inger Larsdatter**, residing in Stavanger, 1817.

added.informant_bias_notes: TENTATIVE SURNAME. Index reads 'Nadnesen,' which is not a recognized Norwegian patronymic. Most probable explanation: OCR or transcription slip of 'Aadnesen' — the leading 'Aa' rendered as 'N' in older Kurrent-influenced handwriting. 'Aadnesen' would derive from the given name Ådne/Aadne, well attested in Rogaland. The subject's own patronymic 'Tollefsen' (son of Tollef/Tollev) corroborates the given name 'Tollev' but does not resolve the surname. Surname must be verified against the original register image before being treated as established.

added.notes: Digitized Norwegian parish register (original church book). FamilySearch index transcribes the father's surname as 'Nadnesen' — flagged as uncertain by the record metadata and by the researcher. 'Nadnesen' is not a recognized Norwegian patronymic; it is likely a mistranscription of 'Aadnesen' (from Ådne/Aadne, a common West-Norwegian given name), where the leading 'Aa' was misread as 'N' in the Kurrent-influenced hand. The given name 'Tollev' is internally consistent with the subject's patronymic 'Tollefsen' (son of Tollef/Tollev). Original register image must be consulted to establish the correct spelling before the father's surname can be treated as established.

warnings.advisory: Skill called MCP tools but the rubric has no dimension name suggesting tool-usage coverage (matched against keywords: ['tool usage', 'tool use', 'tool work', 'tool call', 'argument quality', 'argument-quality', 'response interpretation', 'tool selection', 'mcp tool', 'fixture']). The judge will grade other dimensions but won't score tool work explicitly. Consider adding a tool-usage dimension or renaming an existing one.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: Claude correctly extracted the child's christening (20 July 1817, Stavanger), birth date (11 July 1817), birth place, and the mother (Inger Larsdatter). Critically, Claude correctly identified the father-surname uncertainty. Rather than finalize 'Nadnesen' as established fact, Claude flagged it as [Nadnesen?] in the assertion value, documented it as tentative in the informant_bias_notes, and explained the likely mistranscription (Aa→N in Kurrent script). All extracted facts are supported by the record_read response and the research metadata.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: Claude addressed all required components: (1) extracted the child's facts (name, christening date/place, birth date/place), (2) extracted mother facts (name, residence), (3) extracted father facts (given name, residence, relationship), (4) properly escalated the suspect required identifier by marking the surname tentative, (5) logged the fetch operation, (6) added the source with full citation and notes explaining the uncertainty, (7) documented the outstanding step (original register image must be consulted). Nothing the user requested or the test scenario required was omitted.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: All four MCP tool calls used correct arguments: record_read passed the exact ARK identifier (68Q3-5SGC); research_log_append included the correct projectPath and provided comprehensive query, outcome, and notes fields; tree_edit called add_source with title and URL correctly; research_append passed valid JSON for 11 operations (1 source + 10 assertions) with all required fields properly structured. No fixture_not_found errors; all calls matched their expected patterns.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Each of the 10 assertions extracted a single fact: child's name, christening (as a separate event with date and place decomposed as structured_value), birth date, birth place, father's name, father's residence, father's relationship, mother's name, mother's residence, mother's relationship. Compound information (e.g., christening date + place from the record) was correctly decomposed into separate fact_type entries with distinct values. All facts are independently queryable.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: Claude distinguished between recorder (church officiant) and informants. For child/parent names: 'parents (unnamed, present at baptism)' with informant_proximity 'witness'; for father/mother self-reported names: 'Tollev (father, presumably present)' / 'Inger Larsdatter (mother, present)' with proximity 'self'; for residence and relationship facts: 'church officiant/recorder' with proximity 'official_duty'. The identification is granular and captures proximity accurately for a baptism record where specific household members reported names.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: All evidence_type assignments are correct as 'direct': the christening event, birth date, birth place, names, and relationships are all explicitly stated in the church register (a primary source with direct statement). The residence facts for father (Birkeland) and mother (Stavanger) are direct entries in the record. None require inference from household position or context; no negative evidence is present. The classifications support proper downstream conflict resolution.
